title: Swish & GELU: Smooth Modern Activations
concept: Newer activation functions that are smooth and self-gated (using the input's own sigmoid to scale itself); used in many modern large-scale networks for slightly better performance than ReLU.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what makes ELU's negative-input shape different from a straight sloped line?

options.text: It's a smooth curve that levels off near a small negative value
It jumps straight to a big negative number
It's identical to ReLU's hard cutoff at 0

exercises.explain: Right — ELU curves and levels off. Every function so far picks ONE fixed rule and applies it everywhere. What if a function could decide, input by input, how much of itself to let through?

cards.title: What if the input decided its own fate?
cards.body: So far, every activation function follows one fixed rule for every input: chop negatives, add a slope, curve it. But some of the newest activation functions — used inside cutting-edge large-scale networks — let each input help decide how much of itself gets through.

cards.title: Self-gated: the input scales itself
cards.body: Swish and GELU are both smooth, self-gated activation functions. "Self-gated" means each input value is multiplied by a Sigmoid-like gate calculated from that same input — so the input itself decides, moment to moment, how much of its own signal to let through, rather than following one fixed rule for every value.

cards.title: A gate that reads its own traffic
cards.body: ReLU is like a gate that only checks one thing: is the car (input) positive or negative? Fully open or fully shut, nothing in between. Swish and GELU are gates that also look at how strong the incoming signal is, smoothly opening wider for confident signals and narrowing — but never slamming fully shut — for weak or negative ones.

cards.title: Smooth everywhere, unlike ReLU's sharp bend
cards.body: ReLU has one sharp bend right at 0 — a hard corner. Swish and GELU have no sharp corners anywhere: the whole curve bends smoothly, even right around 0, and can even dip slightly negative for small negative inputs before curving back up — instead of hard-clipping straight to zero.

cards.title: Not just ReLU with extra steps
cards.body: You might assume Swish and GELU are just fancier, slower versions of ReLU with no real payoff. In reality, many modern large-scale networks — including several state-of-the-art language and image models — use Swish or GELU specifically because their smoothness tends to squeeze out slightly better performance than ReLU.

left.text:
Sharp corner at 0
Fixed rule: pass through or block

right.text:
Smooth curve everywhere
Self-gated: input helps scale itself

exercises.prompt: What does it mean for Swish and GELU to be "self-gated"?

options.text: Each input is scaled by a gate calculated from that same input
They only activate every other neuron
They gate the output layer only

exercises.explain: Right — self-gated means the input's own value helps determine how much of it passes through.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Swish and GELU have a sharp corner at 0, just like ReLU.
exercises.explain: False! Swish and GELU are smooth everywhere, with no sharp corners.

exercises.sentence: Swish and GELU are described as smooth and self-___, meaning the input helps scale itself.
exercises.explain: Self-gated: the input's own value determines how much of itself passes through.

exercises.prompt: Which kind of activation function is used in many modern large-scale networks for its smooth, self-gated shape?
exercises.explain: Swish and GELU's smoothness and self-gating make them common choices in modern large-scale networks.

exercises.prompt: A research team building a large modern language model wants an activation function that's smooth everywhere (no sharp corners) and lets each input help scale itself, aiming for a small performance edge over plain ReLU. Which activation function(s) fit this description, and why?

options.text: Swish or GELU, because both are smooth, self-gated functions where the input's own value determines how much of itself passes through
Regular ReLU, because sharp corners always train faster
Softmax, because it's used in every layer of modern networks
Leaky ReLU, because its fixed slope is inherently self-gated

exercises.explain: Exactly! Swish and GELU bring smoothness and self-gating to modern large-scale networks. You've now met eight different activation functions across two units. Time to line them all up and figure out when to reach for each one.
